turns.content: I'm trying to build an automation script in Rust and I keep thinking the migration logic is just like Java's database tools where you run a single command. Why does this even work this way when my code keeps failing because I forgot that sea-orm-migration CLI actually requires me to implement MigrationTrait manually instead of just calling a function?

The core difference is that Java's JDBC tools often handle the SQL generation internally, but in Rust with SeaORM you must define every schema change as a trait object. You need to implement MigrationTrait for each table or column change so the compiler knows what to run. Think of it like building a custom brick wall: Java gives you pre-made bricks that fit together automatically, whereas Rust requires you to design and hand-lay each brick yourself by implementing specific traits before anything gets built.
Okay so I just need to write a trait implementation for every schema change? But what if I want to undo the changes later since my automation script needs to be reversible?

You can handle reversibility by implementing both forward and backward migrations as separate traits. For example, you might create a Down migration trait alongside your Up trait so that when your automation tool runs the rollback phase, it executes those down implementations instead of trying to re-run the up ones. This way your CLI can orchestrate the full lifecycle of schema changes without losing track of what was deployed.
